content:
{
  "title": "Graph-Augmented Prompting for Structured Mathematical Problem Solving in Large Language Models",
  "problem": "Current prompting techniques for LLMs struggle with complex multi-step mathematical problems requiring structured, interdependent reasoning. These models often fail to capture the underlying dependency graph among mathematical subproblems within a single prompt.",
  "motivation": "Improving the structured reasoning of LLMs is critical for handling real-world math challenges, such as competition problems and research-level proofs, where subproblems are deeply interlinked. Existing methods, such as chain-of-thought and self-verification, inadequately model the compositional dependencies between intermediate results, limiting systematic error detection and correction.",
  "method": "We propose Graph-Augmented Prompting (GAP), a novel prompting method which explicitly encodes the dependency graph of subproblems within mathematical prompts. Users annotate math problems with nodes (subproblems) and directed edges (dependency relations), and the prompt instructs the LLM to solve nodes in topological order, referencing prior computed results as needed. The LLM is prompted to output all intermediate results, track dependencies, and propagate corrections backward through the graph if inconsistencies are found. This approach leverages LLM reasoning while imposing an explicit graph-structured workflow.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate GAP on GSM8K and MATH datasets, comparing against chain-of-thought, self-consistency, and multi-agent debate baselines. Metrics include exact match accuracy, intermediate consistency, and subproblem-level correction rates. We will ablate the effects of dependency annotation and graph-based correction."
}
